content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 24

**Chapter:** Leviticus 24 (23 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Agents compared:** EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Total disagreements:** 47

---

## Disagreement Index

| # | Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | Ruling |
|---|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|--------|
| 1 | 1 | Name form: Moses | Moshe | Moses | Moses | Moses |
| 2 | 1–2 | Verse division | Separate vv. | Separate vv. | Combined vv. 1–2 | Separate |
| 3 | 2 | Community address formula | "sons of Yisra'el" | "sons of Israel" | "the Israelites" | "sons of Israel" |
| 4 | 2 | Lamp verb | "to cause a lamp to go up" | "to cause a lamp to burn" | "so that the lamp can be kept burning" | "to cause a lamp to go up" |
| 5 | 3 | "Veil" vocabulary | "veil of the testimony" | "veil of the testimony" | "curtain that screens the Covenant chest" | "veil of the testimony" |
| 6 | 3 | Permanent-ordinance formula | "statute forever throughout your generations" | "statute forever throughout your generations" | "permanent ordinance for all your generations" | "statute forever throughout your generations" |
| 7 | 4 | Menorah: "gold" | "pure lampstand" | "pure gold lampstand" | "pure gold lampstand" | "pure lampstand" |
| 8 | 5 | Measure specification | "two-tenths" | "two tenths of an ephah" | "two-tenths of an ephah" | "two-tenths of an ephah" |
| 9 | 5 | "Each loaf" vs. "the one loaf" | "the one loaf" | "each loaf" | "each loaf" | "each loaf" |
| 10 | 6 | "Six to a row" vs. "six in a row" | "six to a row" | "six in a row" | "six in each row" | "six to a row" |
| 11 | 6 | Table: "gold" | "pure table" | "pure gold table" | "pure gold table" | "pure table" |
| 12 | 7 | Frankincense placement | "upon each row" | "on each row" | "alongside each row" | "upon each row" |
| 13 | 7 | Memorial-portion clause | "shall be for the bread as a memorial portion" | "that it may go with the bread as a memorial portion" | "to serve as a token portion…in place of the bread" | "that it may serve as a memorial portion for the bread" |
| 14 | 7 | "Fire-offering" vs. "food offering" | "fire-offering" | "food offering" | "fire-offering" | "fire-offering" |
| 15 | 8 | Sabbath doubling | literal doubling: "on the sabbath day, on the sabbath day" | "Every Sabbath day" | "Every Sabbath" | "Every Sabbath day" |
| 16 | 8 | "Covenant forever" wording | "everlasting covenant" | "a covenant forever" | "everlasting covenant, a commitment on the part of the Israelites" | "a covenant forever" |
| 17 | 9 | "Most holy to/for him" | "most holy for him" | "most holy to him" | "most holy — his portion" | "most holy to him" |
| 18 | 9 | "Fire-offerings" vs. "food offerings" | "fire-offerings of the LORD" | "the LORD's food offerings" | "fire-offerings of the LORD" | "fire-offerings of the LORD" |
| 19 | 9 | Final formula | "a statute forever" | "a perpetual due" | "given to him as a permanent right" | "a statute forever" |
| 20 | 10 | Narrative opening | "And the son of a woman of Yisra'el went out" | "Now a man who was the son of an Israelite woman went out" | "Now a man whose mother was an Israelite…came out" | "Now the son of a woman of Israel went out" |
| 21 | 10 | Fight vocabulary | "strove together" | "quarreled" | "a fight broke out" | "strove together" |
| 22 | 10 | "In the camp" placement | end of v. 10 | end of v. 10 | as separate sentence at start of description | end of v. 10 |
| 23 | 11 | "Cursed" object | "cursed" (no object) | "cursed" (no object) | "cursed it" | "cursed" (no object) |
| 24 | 11 | "Daughter of Dibri" article | "daughter of Divri" | "the daughter of Dibri" | "daughter of Dibri" | "the daughter of Dibri" |
| 25 | 12 | Custody term | "under guard" | "in custody" | "in custody" | "under guard" |
| 26 | 12 | Purpose clause rendering | "to make a decision according to the mouth of the LORD" | "until the will of the LORD should be plain to them" | "while they waited to hear what the LORD would say" | "until the will of the LORD should be made plain to them" |
| 27 | 14 | Imperative: "bring out" vs. "bring" | "Bring out" (hotse) | "Bring…outside the camp" | "Bring…outside the camp" | "Bring out…to the outside of the camp" |
| 28 | 14 | "One who cursed" — epithet | "the one who cursed [ha-meqallel]" | "the one who has cursed" | "the one who has done the cursing" | "the one who cursed" |
| 29 | 14 | Jussive / imperative mood | "all those who heard… shall lay" | "let all who heard him lay" | "Let all who heard him lay" | "let all who heard him lay" |
| 30 | 14 | "Entire congregation" vs. "all the congregation" | "entire congregation" | "all the congregation" | "the whole community" | "entire congregation" |
| 31 | 15 | Universal subject | "any man [ish ish]" | "Whoever" | "Anyone" | "Anyone" |
| 32 | 16 | Verb for *naqav* in legal formula | "he who pierces the Name of the LORD" | "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD" | "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD" | "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD" |
| 33 | 16 | "Sojourner…native" vs. "foreigner…native-born Israelite" | "sojourner…native" | "sojourner…native" | "foreigner…native-born Israelite" | "sojourner…native" |
| 34 | 17 | "Strikes fatally" vs. "takes…life" | "strikes fatally any person" | "takes a human life" | "takes a human life" | "takes a human life" |
| 35 | 18 | "Strikes fatally an animal" vs. "takes…life" | "strikes fatally an animal" | "takes an animal's life" | "kills another person's animal" | "takes the life of an animal" |
| 36 | 18 | "Make restitution" vs. "make it good" | "make restitution for it" | "make it good" | "make full restitution" | "make restitution for it" |
| 37 | 19 | "Inflicts a blemish" vs. "injures" | "inflicts a blemish [mum]" | "injures" | "injures" | "inflicts a blemish" |
| 38 | 19 | "His fellow" vs. "his neighbor" | "his fellow [amito]" | "his neighbor" | "his neighbor" | "his neighbor" |
| 39 | 20 | "As he inflicted a blemish" vs. "whatever injury" | "as he inflicted a blemish upon a person [mum]…so shall it be inflicted upon him" | "Whatever injury he has given a person shall be given to him" | "The same injury he has inflicted on someone else must be inflicted on him" | "As he inflicted a blemish on a person, so shall it be inflicted on him" |
| 40 | 21 | Summary verb — animal | "strikes an animal" | "kills an animal" | "kills an animal" | "kills an animal" |
| 41 | 21 | "Make restitution" vs. "make it good" | "make restitution for it" | "make it good" | "make full restitution" | "make restitution for it" |
| 42 | 22 | "One law" vs. "one standard of justice" | "one law" | "one law" | "one standard of justice" | "one law" |
| 43 | 22 | "Sojourner…native" (second occurrence) | "sojourner…native" | "sojourner…native" | "foreigner…native-born" | "sojourner…native" |
| 44 | 23 | "Brought…to the outside of the camp" | "to the outside of the camp" | "outside the camp" | "outside the camp" | "to the outside of the camp" |
| 45 | 23 | "Stoned him with stones" | "stoned him with stones" | "stoned him with stones" | "stoned him" | "stoned him with stones" |
| 46 | 23 | Compliance subject | "sons of Yisra'el" | "the sons of Israel" | "The Israelites" | "the sons of Israel" |
| 47 | 23 | "Exactly" addition | absent | absent | "did exactly what" | absent — not in MT |

---

## Detailed Disagreement Records

**#1 — V. 1 — Name form: Moses / Moshe**
- EMET: "Moshe" (Hebrew transliteration throughout)
- LOGOS: "Moses" (English form)
- PNEUMA: "Moses" (English form)
- Ruling: "Moses" — project convention for English name forms in the consensus text. EMET's transliterated form is consistent with its register but is not the consensus standard. Applies also at vv. 13, 23.

**#2 — Vv. 1–2 — Verse division**
- EMET: Treats vv. 1 and 2 as separate numbered verses.
- LOGOS: Treats vv. 1 and 2 as separate numbered verses.
- PNEUMA: Merges vv. 1 and 2 into a single block ("The LORD spoke to Moses: 'Command the Israelites to bring you…'"), eliminating the formal address formula as a separate unit.
- Ruling: Separate verses per MT verse numbering. The address formula ("And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying") is a distinct verse-level unit in the Masoretic Text and must be preserved as v. 1; the commanded content begins at v. 2.

**#3 — V. 2 — Community address formula**
- EMET: "sons of Yisra'el" (transliterated)
- LOGOS: "sons of Israel"
- PNEUMA: "the Israelites"
- Ruling: "sons of Israel" — the project-established rendering of *bney yisrael* throughout Leviticus. PNEUMA's "the Israelites" is acceptable English but loses the covenant-community designation "sons of," which recurs as a formal legal subject throughout the Holiness Code.

**#4 — V. 2 — Verb for tending the lamp: "go up" / "burn" / "kept burning"**
- EMET: "to cause a lamp to go up [le-ha'alot ner]" — Hiphil of *'alah*, "to cause to ascend"
- LOGOS: "to cause a lamp to burn continually" — interpretive gloss on the verb's purpose
- PNEUMA: "so that the lamp can be kept burning continually" — paraphrastic, restructures the clause
- Ruling: "to cause a lamp to go up" — EMET. The Hiphil *he'elot* is the technical sanctuary idiom for lighting/tending a lamp so the flame "ascends." "Burn" (LOGOS) and "kept burning" (PNEUMA) describe the result rather than the act specified by the verb. The technical term *he'elot ner* should be preserved in the consensus as a sanctuary-specific cultic expression.

**#5 — V. 3 — "Veil" vs. "curtain that screens the Covenant chest"**
- EMET: "veil of the testimony [parokhet ha-edut]"
- LOGOS: "veil of the testimony"
- PNEUMA: "curtain that screens the Covenant chest"
- Ruling: "veil of the testimony" — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA substitutes a functional description ("that screens the Covenant chest") for the technical sanctuary term *parokhet ha-edut*. Both components — *parokhet* (the inner curtain/veil) and *ha-edut* (the testimony, referring to the tablets and their ark) — are technical Tabernacle vocabulary that should be preserved.

**#6 — V. 3 — "Permanent ordinance" vs. "statute forever"**
- EMET: "a statute forever [chuqqat olam] throughout your generations"
- LOGOS: "It shall be a statute forever throughout your generations"
- PNEUMA: "This is a permanent ordinance for all your generations"
- Ruling: "a statute forever throughout your generations" — EMET/LOGOS. *Chuqqat olam* is the project-established formula (cf. Lev 16:29, 31, 34; 17:7; 23:14, 21, 31, 41; 24:3, 9). PNEUMA's "permanent ordinance" substitutes a non-technical English phrase; "statute forever" preserves the legal category (*chuqqah*) and the temporal scope (*olam*).

**#7 — V. 4 — Menorah: "pure lampstand" vs. "pure gold lampstand"**
- EMET: "the pure lampstand [ha-menorah ha-tehorah]"
- LOGOS: "the pure gold lampstand" — harmonizing with Exod 25 and the LOGOS note citation of *menorat ha-zahav ha-tahor*
- PNEUMA: "the pure gold lampstand"
- Ruling: "the pure lampstand" — EMET. The MT at v. 4 reads *al-ha-menorah ha-tehorah* — "on the pure lampstand" — without the word *zahav* ("gold"). LOGOS and PNEUMA add "gold" from harmonization with the broader tabernacle tradition; the consensus follows the MT text at this verse. The menorah's gold composition is documented elsewhere (Exod 25:31; 37:17) and is implied by *ha-tehorah*, but it is not present in the Hebrew of this specific verse.

**#8 — V. 5 — "Two tenths of an ephah" — specification**
- EMET: "two-tenths [shnei esronim]" (without "of an ephah")
- LOGOS: "two tenths of an ephah"
- PNEUMA: "two-tenths of an ephah"
- Ruling: "two-tenths of an ephah" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. Adding "of an ephah" is standard clarification for the *esron* measurement (one-tenth of an *ephah*) and is not an addition to the meaning; it simply makes the measure intelligible to an English reader. The bare "two-tenths" (EMET) without the unit is opaque.

**#9 — V. 5 — "The one loaf" vs. "each loaf"**
- EMET: "the one loaf [ha-challah ha-echat]" (literal rendering of the Hebrew article + numeral)
- LOGOS: "each loaf"
- PNEUMA: "each loaf"
- Ruling: "each loaf" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT *ha-challah ha-echat* ("the one loaf") uses the singular distributively — "for each [one] loaf." "Each loaf" is the natural English idiom for this distributive construction; "the one loaf" is a wooden literal rendering that could imply only a single loaf is in view.

**#10 — V. 6 — "Six to a row" vs. "six in a row" vs. "six in each row"**
- EMET: "six to a row [sheshet ha-ma'arakah]"
- LOGOS: "six in a row"
- PNEUMA: "six in each row"
- Ruling: "six to a row" — EMET. The MT *sheshet ha-ma'arakah* is a terse partitive phrase meaning "six per arrangement." "Six to a row" best captures the partitive sense. "Six in a row" (LOGOS) could imply sequential order along a single row. "Six in each row" (PNEUMA) adds "each" not present in the Hebrew.

**#11 — V. 6 — Table: "pure table" vs. "pure gold table"**
- EMET: "the pure table [ha-shulchan ha-tahor]"
- LOGOS: "the pure gold table"
- PNEUMA: "the pure gold table"
- Ruling: "the pure table" — EMET. The MT *ha-shulchan ha-tahor* does not include *zahav* ("gold") at this verse. Parallel to the ruling at #7 (v. 4, the menorah). LOGOS and PNEUMA again harmonize with the broader tabernacle tradition; the consensus follows the MT.

**#12 — V. 7 — "Upon each row" vs. "on each row" vs. "alongside each row"**
- EMET: "upon each row [al-ha-ma'arakah]"
- LOGOS: "on each row"
- PNEUMA: "alongside each row"
- Ruling: "upon each row" — EMET. The preposition *al* means "upon/on." PNEUMA's "alongside" is an interpretive decision about the physical placement of the frankincense bowls; while archaeologically plausible, "alongside" is not what the preposition *al* means. "Upon" is the literal and the preferred rendering.

**#13 — V. 7 — Memorial-portion clause: structural divergence**
- EMET: "it shall be for the bread as a memorial portion [le-azkarah], a fire-offering to the LORD"
- LOGOS: "that it may go with the bread as a memorial portion, a food offering to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "This is to serve as a token portion, a fire-offering to the LORD in place of the bread"
- Ruling: "that it may serve as a memorial portion for the bread, a fire-offering to the LORD." The MT *ve-hayetah la-lechem le-azkarah isheh la-YHWH* — "and it shall be for the bread as a memorial portion, a fire-offering to the LORD." Key decisions: (a) "serve as" (functional *hayetah*) over LOGOS's "go with" (which misreads the relationship — the frankincense does not merely accompany the bread; it *functions* as the bread's memorial portion); (b) "memorial portion" over PNEUMA's "token portion" — *azkarah* is the established project rendering (cf. Lev 2:2, 9, 16); (c) "fire-offering" over LOGOS's "food offering" (see #14).

**#14 — V. 7 — "Fire-offering" vs. "food offering" for *isheh***
- EMET: "fire-offering [isheh]"
- LOGOS: "food offering"
- PNEUMA: "fire-offering"
- Ruling: "fire-offering" — EMET/PNEUMA. The term *isheh* (from *esh*, "fire") is the established project term for the class of offerings involving fire. "Food offering" (LOGOS) reflects a minority scholarly view that *isheh* derives from a root meaning "gift" rather than "fire"; the consensus follows the established *isheh* = "fire-offering" rendering used throughout the project.

**#15 — V. 8 — Sabbath doubling: literal vs. idiomatic**
- EMET: "on the sabbath day, on the sabbath day [be-yom ha-shabbat be-yom ha-shabbat]" — literal doubling
- LOGOS: "Every Sabbath day" — idiomatic equivalent
- PNEUMA: "Every Sabbath" — idiomatic, drops "day"
- Ruling: "Every Sabbath day" — LOGOS. The Hebrew doubling of *be-yom ha-shabbat* is an emphatic idiom meaning "every single Sabbath day / regularly on the Sabbath." The idiomatic "Every Sabbath day" captures the distributive force. EMET's literal repetition is accurate but awkward in English prose. PNEUMA drops "day" (*yom*) from the MT phrase without justification.

**#16 — V. 8 — "Everlasting covenant" vs. "a covenant forever"**
- EMET: "an everlasting covenant [berit olam]"
- LOGOS: "a covenant forever"
- PNEUMA: "an everlasting covenant, a commitment on the part of the Israelites"
- Ruling: "a covenant forever" — LOGOS. For terminological consistency with *chuqqat olam* → "a statute forever" (vv. 3, 9), the parallel formula *berit olam* is rendered "a covenant forever" (not "everlasting covenant"). Both are accurate; the project prefers the -forever pattern for the *olam* compounds in this chapter. PNEUMA's addition "a commitment on the part of the Israelites" has no basis in the MT and is an explanatory insertion.

**#17 — V. 9 — "Most holy for him" vs. "most holy to him" vs. "most holy — his portion"**
- EMET: "most holy for him [qodesh qodashim hu lo]"
- LOGOS: "most holy to him"
- PNEUMA: "most holy — his portion"
- Ruling: "most holy to him" — LOGOS. The MT *qodesh qodashim hu lo* — "holiness of holinesses, it is to/for him." "To him" (LOGOS) is marginally more natural than "for him" (EMET) as the English rendering of the dative *lo*. PNEUMA's "his portion" introduces a term ("portion") not present in the Hebrew *lo* ("to/for him"), converting a holiness designation into a priestly-ration category.

**#18 — V. 9 — "Fire-offerings" vs. "food offerings" (second occurrence)**
- EMET: "from the fire-offerings of the LORD [me-ishei YHWH]"
- LOGOS: "from the LORD's food offerings"
- PNEUMA: "of the fire-offerings of the LORD"
- Ruling: "from the fire-offerings of the LORD" — EMET/PNEUMA. Repeats the ruling at #14. *Ishei YHWH* = "fire-offerings of the LORD." LOGOS's "food offerings" is again a minority rendering of *isheh*.

**#19 — V. 9 — Closing formula: "a statute forever" vs. "a perpetual due" vs. "a permanent right"**
- EMET: "a statute forever [chuqqat olam]"
- LOGOS: "a perpetual due"
- PNEUMA: "given to him as a permanent right"
- Ruling: "a statute forever" — EMET. *Chuqqat olam* at v. 9 matches the identical formula at v. 3. The project convention is "a statute forever" for this phrase throughout. LOGOS's "perpetual due" and PNEUMA's "permanent right" are paraphrases that capture the practical meaning but lose the technical legal-covenantal term *chuqqah* (statute/ordinance).

**#20 — V. 10 — Narrative opening construction**
- EMET: "And the son of a woman of Yisra'el went out… and he was the son of an Egyptian man… among the sons of Yisra'el; and the son of the woman of Yisra'el [va-yinnatsev] and a man of Yisra'el strove together [va-yinnatsu] in the camp"
- LOGOS: "Now a man who was the son of an Israelite woman went out — and his father was an Egyptian man — among the sons of Israel. And the son of the Israelite woman and a man of Israel quarreled in the camp."
- PNEUMA: "Now a man whose mother was an Israelite and whose father was an Egyptian came out among the Israelites. In the camp, a fight broke out between this man of mixed descent and an Israelite."
- Ruling: "Now the son of a woman of Israel went out — and his father was an Egyptian man — among the sons of Israel. And the son of the Israelite woman and a man of Israel strove together in the camp." The MT opens with *va-yetse ben-ishah Yisra'elit* ("and there went out the son of a woman, an Israelite-woman"). LOGOS's restructuring to "a man who was the son of" adds a layer not in the Hebrew. PNEUMA's "man of mixed descent" is an interpretive addition; the text does not use that phrase. EMET's retention of "son of a woman of Israel" is the closest to the MT's presentation. "Strove together" for *va-yinnatsu* over "quarreled" (LOGOS) and "a fight broke out" (PNEUMA) — see #21.

**#21 — V. 10 — Fight vocabulary: "strove together" / "quarreled" / "a fight broke out"**
- EMET: "strove together [va-yinnatsu — Niphal of natsa]"
- LOGOS: "quarreled"
- PNEUMA: "a fight broke out"
- Ruling: "strove together" — EMET. The Niphal of *n-ts-h* (va-yinnatsu) denotes active physical striving/fighting; it appears also in Exod 2:13 ("two Hebrews striving together"). "Quarreled" (LOGOS) undersells the physicality of the Hebrew verb. PNEUMA's "a fight broke out" passivizes the verb, removing the two named individuals as the grammatical agents of the strife.

**#22 — V. 10 — "In the camp" placement**
- EMET: "strove together within the camp [be-tokh ha-machaneh]"
- LOGOS: "quarreled in the camp" — at end of verse
- PNEUMA: "In the camp, a fight broke out…" — at beginning of a separate sentence, restructuring the verse
- Ruling: "strove together in the camp" — at end of verse, following MT. The MT places *be-tokh ha-machaneh* at the end of the verse. PNEUMA's restructuring moves "in the camp" to the beginning of the fight description, disrupting the MT's narrative word order.

**#23 — V. 11 — "Cursed" with or without object**
- EMET: "and he cursed [va-yeqallel]" — no explicit object
- LOGOS: "and cursed" — no object
- PNEUMA: "and cursed it" — adds "it" as object of the cursing
- Ruling: "and cursed" — no object (EMET/LOGOS). The MT *va-yeqallel* is objectless — the cursing is presented without a specified target. PNEUMA's "it" imports the assumption that the Name was the object of both verbs; while this may be the correct understanding, the MT's objectless construction should be preserved in the consensus, leaving the referent implicit.

**#24 — V. 11 — "The daughter of Dibri" — definite article**
- EMET: "daughter of Divri [bat Divri]"
- LOGOS: "the daughter of Dibri"
- PNEUMA: "daughter of Dibri"
- Ruling: "the daughter of Dibri" — LOGOS. The MT *bat Divri* in context is a proper identification ("the daughter of Dibri"), and English idiom uses the definite article when identifying a person by their parent ("the daughter of X"). EMET and PNEUMA omit the article; LOGOS supplies it naturally.

**#25 — V. 12 — Custody term: "under guard" vs. "in custody"**
- EMET: "under guard [ba-mishmar]"
- LOGOS: "in custody"
- PNEUMA: "in custody"
- Ruling: "under guard" — EMET. *Ba-mishmar* is literally "in the place of guarding/watching" (from *shamar*, "to watch/guard"). "Under guard" preserves the specific nuance of *mishmar* as an act of watching/guarding, not merely confinement. "In custody" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the modern equivalent but loses the connection to the Hebrew root and to the parallel use of *mishmar* in Num 15:34 (the Sabbath wood-gatherer case). Project convention has been to preserve technical terms where possible.

**#26 — V. 12 — Purpose clause: "until the will of the LORD should be plain to them"**
- EMET: "to make a decision [lifros lahem] according to the mouth of the LORD [al-pi YHWH]"
- LOGOS: "until the will of the LORD should be plain to them"
- PNEUMA: "while they waited to hear what the LORD would say"
- Ruling: "until the will of the LORD should be made plain to them" — LOGOS adjusted. The MT *lifros lahem al-pi YHWH* is an infinitive purpose clause: "to make clear/explain to them by the mouth of the LORD." LOGOS's "until the will of the LORD should be plain to them" turns this into a temporal clause, which is acceptable and idiomatic. PNEUMA's "while they waited to hear what the LORD would say" is too colloquial and loses the specific verb *paras* ("to explain/make distinct") and the technical formula *al-pi YHWH* ("according to the mouth of the LORD"). The consensus adds "made" before "plain" to activate the implied Hiphil causative sense of *paras*.

**#27 — V. 14 — Imperative: "bring out" vs. "bring"**
- EMET: "Bring out [hotse — Hiphil imperative of yatsa] the one who cursed to the outside of the camp"
- LOGOS: "Bring the one who has cursed outside the camp"
- PNEUMA: "Bring the one who has done the cursing outside the camp"
- Ruling: "Bring out the one who cursed to the outside of the camp" — EMET. The Hiphil imperative *hotse* ("bring out / cause to go out") is directional; the subject is made to exit. LOGOS's plain "Bring" omits the causative direction of the Hiphil. "To the outside of the camp" preserves the MT directional phrase *el-mi-chutz la-machaneh*; LOGOS and PNEUMA render it as "outside the camp" (a state rather than a movement).

**#28 — V. 14 — "The one who cursed" — epithet**
- EMET: "the one who cursed [ha-meqallel]" — Piel participle, past-tense reading
- LOGOS: "the one who has cursed" — perfect tense in English
- PNEUMA: "the one who has done the cursing" — circumlocution
- Ruling: "the one who cursed" — EMET. *Ha-meqallel* is a Piel participle functioning substantivally as a title/epithet for the condemned man, not a present or ongoing action. "The one who cursed" is the most direct rendering; "has cursed" (LOGOS) and "has done the cursing" (PNEUMA) both add unnecessary complexity.

**#29 — V. 14 — Jussive mood for hand-laying**
- EMET: "all those who heard… shall lay their hands upon his head"
- LOGOS: "let all who heard him lay their hands on his head"
- PNEUMA: "Let all who heard him lay their hands on his head"
- Ruling: "let all who heard him lay their hands on his head" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT *ve-samkhu kol-ha-shomim* uses an imperfect with a jussive/permissive force in a divine instruction context. "Let all who heard him lay their hands" captures this permissive-imperative nuance more naturally than EMET's indicative "shall lay."

**#30 — V. 14 — "Entire congregation" vs. "all the congregation" vs. "the whole community"**
- EMET: "the entire congregation [kol-ha-edah]"
- LOGOS: "all the congregation"
- PNEUMA: "the whole community"
- Ruling: "the entire congregation" — EMET. *Kol-ha-edah* is the formal covenant-assembly term used throughout Leviticus and Numbers; "congregation" is the established project rendering of *edah* (cf. Lev 4, 8, 16, 19). "Community" (PNEUMA) is a softer, less theologically specific term that loses the formal covenant-assembly character of *edah*.

**#31 — V. 15 — Universal subject: "any man [ish ish]" vs. "Whoever" vs. "Anyone"**
- EMET: "Any man [ish ish]" — literal rendering of the distributive doubled *ish*
- LOGOS: "Whoever"
- PNEUMA: "Anyone"
- Ruling: "Anyone" — PNEUMA/adjusted. Following the ruling established at Lev 20:9 on *ish ish*: the distributive doubling of *ish* has universal scope, and "anyone" captures that scope without the gender-implicit "any man." "Whoever" (LOGOS) is also acceptable but slightly more formal and potentially restrictive in pronoun use. The consensus uses "Anyone" for clarity and inclusivity of the legal subject.

**#32 — V. 16 — Verb for *naqav* in the legal formula: "pierces" vs. "blasphemes"**
- EMET: "he who pierces the Name of the LORD [noqev Shem YHWH]"
- LOGOS: "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD"
- Ruling: "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. This is one of the most significant divergences in the chapter. EMET's "pierces" preserves the literal physical etymology of *n-q-v* (to bore/pierce), which EMET uses consistently throughout (vv. 11, 16). However, in the context of a formal legal statute, "blasphemes" is the universally understood English term for the offense in view, and it is what the Hebrew *naqav* means in this specific liturgical-legal context. The consensus uses "blasphemes" in the legal formulas (v. 16) and "blasphemed" in the narrative (v. 11) — both translations of *naqav*. The distinction between *naqav* (pierce/blaspheme the Name specifically) and *qillel* (curse/make contemptible) is preserved by using "blasphemes" and "curses" respectively.

**#33 — V. 16 — "Sojourner / native" vs. "foreigner / native-born Israelite"**
- EMET: "the sojourner [ka-ger]… the native [ka-ezrach]"
- LOGOS: "The sojourner as well as the native"
- PNEUMA: "Whether he is a foreigner or a native-born Israelite"
- Ruling: "the sojourner as well as the native" — EMET/LOGOS. *Ger* is a resident alien who lives within the covenant community — not a foreigner who stands outside it. PNEUMA's "foreigner" is too strong an equivalence, carrying connotations of a non-resident outsider. "Native-born Israelite" (PNEUMA) adds "Israelite" to *ezrach* ("native"), which in the MT designates the native-born person of the land without specifying ethnicity. The consensus preserves the more precise "sojourner" and "native."

**#34 — V. 17 — "Strikes fatally any person" vs. "takes a human life"**
- EMET: "a man [ve-ish] who strikes fatally any person [ki yakkeh kol-nefesh adam]"
- LOGOS: "Whoever takes a human life"
- PNEUMA: "Whoever takes a human life"
- Ruling: "Whoever takes a human life" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's "strikes fatally any person" is a more literal rendering of *ki yakkeh kol-nefesh adam* (Hiphil of *n-k-h* + *kol-nefesh adam*), but "takes a human life" is the idiomatic English equivalent for the result of the striking and is standard in formal translation. "Strikes fatally" is awkward; "takes a human life" reads naturally in a legal context and communicates the same meaning.

**#35 — V. 18 — "Strikes fatally an animal" vs. "takes an animal's life" vs. "kills another person's animal"**
- EMET: "the one who strikes fatally an animal [u-makke nefesh-behemah]"
- LOGOS: "Whoever takes an animal's life"
- PNEUMA: "Whoever kills another person's animal"
- Ruling: "Whoever takes the life of an animal" — adjusted. Parallel to #34: "takes the life of an animal" maintains formal consistency with v. 17 ("takes a human life"). PNEUMA's "another person's animal" adds ownership not present in the MT at this verse; the restitution requirement implies ownership but the Hebrew does not specify it here.

**#36 — V. 18 — "Make restitution for it" vs. "make it good" vs. "make full restitution"**
- EMET: "shall make restitution for it [yeshallemennah — Piel of shillem]"
- LOGOS: "shall make it good"
- PNEUMA: "must make full restitution"
- Ruling: "shall make restitution for it" — EMET. The Piel of *shillem* is the standard restitution term in the Holiness Code. "Make restitution" has been the established project rendering throughout Leviticus; "make it good" (LOGOS) is more colloquial and less precise in legal context. PNEUMA's "full restitution" adds "full" with no basis in the MT.

**#37 — V. 19 — "Inflicts a blemish" vs. "injures"**
- EMET: "inflicts a blemish [yitten mum] in his fellow"
- LOGOS: "injures his neighbor"
- PNEUMA: "injures his neighbor"
- Ruling: "inflicts a blemish on his neighbor" — EMET base adjusted. The MT *yitten mum ba-amito* is literally "gives/inflicts a *mum* (blemish/defect) upon his fellow." *Mum* is the technical Levitical term for physical defects (Lev 21:17–21; 22:20–25) and its use in the lex talionis is deliberate: injury reduces a person to the status of one bearing a *mum*. LOGOS and PNEUMA's "injures" loses this technical vocabulary. The consensus preserves "blemish" for *mum* at vv. 19 and 20.

**#38 — V. 19 — "His fellow" vs. "his neighbor"**
- EMET: "his fellow [ba-amito — amito from 'amit]"
- LOGOS: "his neighbor"
- PNEUMA: "his neighbor"
- Ruling: "his neighbor" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. *Amit* ("fellow/associate/neighbor") has been rendered "neighbor" in the Holiness Code throughout Leviticus (cf. Lev 19:11, 13, 15, 17; 20:10). Consistency with the established project term for *amit* requires "neighbor" here.

**#39 — V. 20 — "As he inflicted a blemish" vs. "whatever injury"**
- EMET: "as he inflicted a blemish upon a person [ka'asher yitten mum ba-adam], so shall it be inflicted upon him [ken yinnaten bo]"
- LOGOS: "Whatever injury he has given a person shall be given to him"
- PNEUMA: "The same injury he has inflicted on someone else must be inflicted on him"
- Ruling: "As he inflicted a blemish on a person, so shall it be inflicted on him" — EMET. The MT repeats *mum* from v. 19: *ka'asher yitten mum ba-adam* — "as he gives/inflicts a *mum* upon a person." For terminological consistency between vv. 19 and 20, *mum* must remain "blemish" in both verses. LOGOS and PNEUMA both use "injury," breaking the terminological link. The passive *yinnaten bo* is rendered "shall it be inflicted on him."

**#40 — V. 21 — Summary verb for animal: "strikes" vs. "kills"**
- EMET: "he who strikes an animal [u-makke behemah]" — Hiphil participle of *n-k-h*
- LOGOS: "Whoever kills an animal"
- PNEUMA: "Whoever kills an animal"
- Ruling: "Whoever kills an animal" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. In this summary context (parallel to the summary clause on persons: *u-makke adam yumat* — "and one who strikes a person shall be put to death"), the Hiphil of *n-k-h* refers to a fatal strike. "Kills" is the natural English equivalent for a fatal blow to an animal in this context, and matches the parallel. "Strikes" alone (EMET) could be ambiguous (not every strike kills).

**#41 — V. 21 — "Make restitution" vs. "make it good" vs. "make full restitution" (second occurrence)**
- EMET: "shall make restitution for it"
- LOGOS: "shall make it good"
- PNEUMA: "must make full restitution"
- Ruling: "shall make restitution for it" — EMET. Repeats ruling at #36. "Make restitution for it" is the established project term for *yeshallemennah*.

**#42 — V. 22 — "One law" vs. "one standard of justice"**
- EMET: "one law [mishpat echad]"
- LOGOS: "one law"
- PNEUMA: "one standard of justice"
- Ruling: "one law" — EMET/LOGOS. *Mishpat echad* is the chapter's theological climax. "One law" is the literal translation and preserves both components: *mishpat* ("law/judgment") and *echad* ("one/single"). PNEUMA's "one standard of justice" expands the Hebrew: "standard" and "of justice" are both additions to the single noun *mishpat*. The weight of *mishpat echad* — the singularity of the covenant legal standard mirroring the singularity of the covenant God — is best preserved by the two-word literal rendering.

**#43 — V. 22 — "Sojourner / native" (second occurrence)**
- EMET: "for the sojourner [la-ger] and for the native [ve-la-ezrach]"
- LOGOS: "for the sojourner and for the native"
- PNEUMA: "the foreigner as to the native-born"
- Ruling: "for the sojourner and for the native" — EMET/LOGOS. Repeats ruling at #33. PNEUMA's "foreigner" and "native-born" are rejected for the same reasons.

**#44 — V. 23 — "To the outside of the camp" vs. "outside the camp"**
- EMET: "to the outside of the camp [el-mi-chutz la-machaneh]"
- LOGOS: "outside the camp"
- PNEUMA: "outside the camp"
- Ruling: "to the outside of the camp" — EMET. The MT directional phrase *el-mi-chutz la-machaneh* ("to the outside of the camp") is present in the compliance report (v. 23) just as it was in the command (v. 14). Preserving the exact phrase in both verses shows that the obedience matched the instruction word-for-word. "Outside the camp" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is a locative state; "to the outside of the camp" is the directional movement the MT specifies.

**#45 — V. 23 — "Stoned him with stones" vs. "stoned him"**
- EMET: "stoned him with stones [va-yirgemu oto even]"
- LOGOS: "stoned him with stones"
- PNEUMA: "stoned him"
- Ruling: "stoned him with stones" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT *even* ("stone") is present as the instrument-accusative in v. 23. PNEUMA drops the instrumental specification. Parallel to the ruling established at Lev 20:2 and 20:27 on "stone him with stones."

**#46 — V. 23 — Compliance subject: "sons of Israel" vs. "the Israelites"**
- EMET: "sons of Yisra'el [benei Yisra'el]"
- LOGOS: "the sons of Israel"
- PNEUMA: "The Israelites"
- Ruling: "the sons of Israel" — LOGOS. Project-established rendering of *bney yisrael*; PNEUMA's "the Israelites" is a shortening that loses the covenant-community formula. See ruling #3.

**#47 — V. 23 — "Exactly" — addition not in MT**
- EMET: "did as the LORD commanded Moses [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe]"
- LOGOS: "did as the LORD had commanded Moses"
- PNEUMA: "did exactly what the LORD had commanded Moses"
- Ruling: "did as the LORD had commanded Moses" — LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "exactly" before "what," intensifying the compliance clause; "exactly" has no counterpart in the MT *ka'asher* ("as/just as"). While the addition is theologically appropriate, it is not in the Hebrew and should not be added to the consensus text. LOGOS's "had commanded" (past perfect) correctly renders the temporal relationship between the earlier command and the present action.

---

## Summary Notes

**Unanimous agreements:** vv. 1 and 13 (address formulas), v. 15b ("shall bear his sin"), v. 16a (mot yumat formula), v. 18b ("life for life"), v. 20a (talionic triad: "fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth"), v. 21b ("whoever kills a person shall be put to death"), v. 22b ("for I am the LORD your God"), and the core rendering "blasphemed the Name and cursed" at v. 11.

**Most contested terms:** (1) *isheh* (vv. 7, 9) — "fire-offering" vs. LOGOS's "food offering"; (2) *mum* (vv. 19–20) — "blemish" vs. "injury"; (3) *ha-menorah ha-tehorah* and *ha-shulchan ha-tahor* (vv. 4, 6) — "gold" addition by LOGOS/PNEUMA not in MT; (4) *naqav* (vv. 11, 16) — EMET's "pierces" vs. consensus "blasphemes"; (5) *mishpat echad* (v. 22) — PNEUMA's "one standard of justice" vs. consensus "one law."

**PNEUMA-specific patterns:** PNEUMA consistently (a) adds modifiers not in the MT ("full," "exactly," "another person's," "of mixed descent"); (b) uses "foreigner" where the MT has *ger* (sojourner); (c) drops "with stones" from stoning formulas; (d) merges verse divisions. All such additions and omissions are corrected in the consensus.

**LOGOS-specific patterns:** LOGOS consistently (a) adds "gold" to the menorah and table descriptions not in the MT at these verses; (b) uses "food offering" for *isheh*; (c) uses "make it good" for *shillem*. All corrected to follow MT and project conventions.

**EMET-specific patterns:** EMET consistently (a) uses transliterated Hebrew name forms (Moshe, Yisra'el, Aharon, Divri, Shelomit); (b) preserves the literal doubling of "on the sabbath day, on the sabbath day"; (c) uses "pierces" for *naqav* throughout. Name forms are adjusted to English convention; the sabbath doubling is rendered idiomatically; "pierces" in the legal formula is replaced by "blasphemes."

---

*Disagreement log complete. Leviticus 24 — 47 disagreements recorded across 23 verses.*
